mnemonics.title: Four Color Theorem
mnemonics.content: FOUR = For Ordinaryplanar Unambiguous Regions. Remember: 4 colours for any map (planar graph). Appel's 4 colours cover all planar maps.

mnemonics.title: Chromatic Number of Complete Graph
mnemonics.content: Kₙ has χ = n because Knit (Kₙ) needs n colours - each vertex needs its own unique colour since all are connected to all.

mnemonics.title: Bipartite = 2-Colourable
mnemonics.content: BIPARTITE = Binary Partition → 2 Colours. Think: Split into two groups (partitions), give each group a colour.

mnemonics.title: Odd Cycle = 3 Colours
mnemonics.content: ODD = Only Default is 3. All odd cycles (C₃, C₅, C₇...) need 3 colours, even cycles need only 2.

acronyms.expansion: Chi of G - Chromatic number of graph G
acronyms.usage: Used to denote minimum colours needed for proper colouring of graph G

acronyms.expansion: Delta of G - Maximum degree of graph G
acronyms.usage: Upper bound for chromatic number in most cases; greedy colouring uses Δ(G)+1 colours worst case

acronyms.expansion: Omega of G - Clique number of graph G
acronyms.usage: Size of largest complete subgraph; always ≤ χ(G)
Counts number of proper k-colourings of graph G

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with all vertices on circumference, connect every pair. Visualize: need n different colours, one for each point on the circle.
Draw vertices in two clear columns (partitions). All edges go from left column to right column - no edges within columns.
Number vertices 1,2,3... in order. When colouring vertex i, look at already coloured neighbours and pick smallest unused colour.
Imagine a map of countries. Adjacent countries (sharing border, not just point) need different colours. Four colours suffice for any map.

analogies.analogy: Think of graph colouring like assigning time slots in a schedule. Adjacent vertices (conflicting events) can't share the same colour (time slot). We want minimum time slots.
Chromatic number is like the minimum number of guards needed to watch all intersections where edges meet, with guards at adjacent intersections being different people.
Colouring a map of countries where neighbouring countries must have different colours - this is exactly the problem the Four Color Theorem solves.
Filling seats in a theater: assign the first available seat number to each person that doesn't conflict with their neighbours' preferences.
